Subject: Proposal for Oral History Evening in September

Respected Members of the District Cultural Committee,

I hope this message finds you in good health and high spirits. I am writing to suggest holding a special oral history evening in early September, where elders of our village may share tales from the old days, accompanied by traditional poetry recitations.

In my view, such a gathering would help our younger generation to hear directly the voices of those who witnessed the village’s transformation over the decades. I would be most willing to help coordinate the event, and can provide my collection of old photographs for display.

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Values equality among all ages above respect for elders.
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me, my friend, has there been a time in our history when the young and old truly had the same say in matters of the village?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, Mehmet Bey, such a question touches both the historian and the villager in me, for the answer depends on where and when you look, and also on what you mean by 'the same say.' In many of our Anatolian villages — just as in the older Punjabi panchayat traditions your forebears would have seen — respect for elders was indeed the backbone of governance. The wisdom of the old was like the reservoir in a dry summer: without it, survival seemed uncertain. Yet, there have been moments and places where the voices of the young were not merely tolerated, but sought as equals.

For example, in certain Anatolian aşiret (tribal) councils of the 18th and 19th centuries, a young herder or apprentice could speak in the meeting if he had proven himself knowledgeable in his duties or if the matter touched the future directly — such as planning migration routes or managing new irrigation methods. In these cases, experience and energy stood side by side.

Similarly, in the Ottoman guild system — the lonca — decisions about craft standards or market days were sometimes made in ortak meclis (joint councils) where master craftsmen sat alongside newer journeymen, each having a single vote, because the survival of the trade relied as much on youthful skill as on matured expertise. I have read records from Edirne in the 1760s where such votes were literally split across age lines yet decided without hierarchy.

Outside our own lands, there are examples too: among some Central Asian nomadic groups, seasonal councils would invite not just elders, but also younger warriors and even unmarried women to discuss route changes. Equality in these moments was not a permanent state — it was practical fairness born from the need to adapt.

So, there were indeed seasons in our history — short, sometimes fleeting — when the young were not merely listeners, and the old were not merely rulers. Equality in voice appeared when the matter at hand concerned everyone’s tomorrow, not just the elders’ yesterday. Perhaps it teaches us that age brings wisdom, but not always monopoly; youth brings energy, but not always enough memory of the road already walked. True balance is when both walk it together, speaking in turn, listening not to the number of years, but to the weight of the words."}, {"role": "user", "content": "And tell me, were these moments of shared voice celebrated, or were they seen as unusual, even dangerous to the old order?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, that is where the heart of the matter lies. In many cases, such moments were appreciated in the moment — for they solved pressing problems — but they were rarely enshrined as the norm. Among the Anatolian aşiret councils I mentioned, a young voice that helped avert disaster would be praised openly, sometimes even honored with a new role or a symbolic gift. Yet, once the immediate crisis passed, the elders often resumed their dominant role. To them, shared voice was a tool for survival, not a permanent arrangement.

In the lonca, younger journeymen who voted alongside masters might be remembered with respect for their good judgment, but the guild’s rules still placed authority in the elders’ hands. If a young member pressed too hard for equal say outside of specific matters, it could be seen as boldness bordering on disrespect.

Only in certain nomadic contexts, where adaptability was life itself, did equal say become a recurring custom. Even then, there was a balance: anyone — young or old — who spoke out of turn or without substance risked losing credibility quickly.

So, Mehmet Bey, equality of voice was not feared so much as it was contained. It was like the spring flood — welcomed for the life it brought, yet guided back into channels before it could change the course of the river entirely."}]

preference: Wears traditional Eastern-style robes and garments
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Evladım, I was wondering… why did our elders insist on those wide, flowing sleeves in the old kaftans when the work of the day could have been done with something simpler?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, Mehmet Rafiq Bey, what you are asking touches not only on cloth and stitch, but on the threads that bind a people’s memory to their hands and hearts. Those wide, flowing sleeves were never merely about covering the arm — they carried the dignity of ease, the quiet message that time was once slower, and movements were meant to be graceful rather than hurried. 

In the courts of the Ottomans, and even in the humbler courtyards of our Anatolian and Central Asian villages, a sleeve could speak of one’s station. Flowing fabric announced that the wearer did not need to rush with tools and burdens all day — it was a silent badge of learning, of hospitality, of the ability to sit and converse without dust on the cuffs. In colder seasons, those folds trapped warmth; in summer, the loose silk or cotton let air circulate gently. And let us not forget the practical poetry of them: a merchant could discreetly keep small scrolls or letters folded within, a host could draw a gift from his sleeve as easily as drawing a smile to his face. 

The elders, you see, understood that clothing taught manners. When your garment moves like water, you too begin to move with consideration, lest you spill it. That is why even the village tailor, though he could stitch something plain and narrow, would keep the sleeve wide — for he knew he was sewing not just apparel, but a certain way of being.

So while it might seem simpler to take the narrower cut, the old kaftan sleeves were part of an unspoken education in composure, hospitality, and grace… lessons stitched into life itself."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Does not watch documentaries on Japanese architecture
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me, evladım, in those old Japanese wooden houses, why did they leave so much empty space inside — was it just for beauty, or something deeper?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, Mehmet Bey, the empty spaces you are thinking of are no accident at all. In traditional Japanese wooden houses — especially those influenced by the sukiya-zukuri and shoin-zukuri styles of the Edo period — the openness was as purposeful as the beams that held up the roof. Part of it was practical: Japan's humid summers and risk of earthquakes called for lightweight wooden frames, sliding doors (fusuma and shōji), and rooms that could open to allow air to flow or be reconfigured depending on need. But the deeper reason, the one that speaks to the heart, comes from their way of seeing the world.

Influenced by Zen Buddhism, Shinto beliefs, and a respect for impermanence, they felt that emptiness was not *nothing*, but a space for life to happen — for a family gathering, for a seasonal tokonoma display, for sunlight falling in a different pattern each hour. A sparse room could be filled by voices, by the aroma of tea, or even simply by the presence of someone thinking quietly. Where in much of Anatolia we fill our rooms with cushions, kilims, and cupboards, they left room for passing breezes and the moment itself to be the decoration.

It also created flexibility. A single wide room could serve as a dining space at noon, a sleeping space by night, or an audience chamber for a guest — all with the slide of a panel and the laying out of a few tatami mats. In this sense, beauty and function were never separate; the idea was that the uncluttered mind often begins in an uncluttered room.

Perhaps that is why, even when one no longer seeks out all the long television explorations of such houses, a small question about them can still carry the scent of pine wood and the whisper of paper doors in the mind."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Prefers classics over contemporary novels
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Why do some old stories seem to grow richer with time, while others fade away?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "That is a question with roots as deep as an old plane tree, Mehmet Bey. Stories, like orchards, are planted in the soil of their time — but some take hold in the deeper strata of human feeling, where seasons and fashions cannot reach.

Part of it is the themes they carry. Tales that touch on the constants of human life — love that defies circumstance, the ache of exile, pride and downfall, the longing for justice — these are not bound to the year they were written. A Hacı Bektaş Veli parable, a verse from Fuzûlî, or Ghalib’s melancholy couplets in Urdu — they all still breathe because the emotions in them are still walking among us.

Then there is the craft of the telling. In many enduring works, the language holds a music of its own: metaphors that open like pomegranates, imagery so vivid you can almost smell the market or feel the dust on a traveller’s cloak. Such expressions are not merely read; they are tasted, remembered, and retold. And with each retelling, they gather new shades of meaning — much as the patina on old copper grows richer over decades.

Another reason is cultural memory. Societies carry certain works forward — by teaching them in schools, rehearsing them in theatre, quoting them at weddings or funerals. These stories become part of a people’s shared conscience. A novel like Halide Edib’s *Ateşten Gömlek* may have been born in a specific national struggle, yet for each new reader, it can also stand as a mirror for personal courage and sacrifice.

And still, some stories fade simply because the thread between teller and listener breaks. Perhaps the language has shifted so far that the words feel foreign, or the society has drifted from the values the work upholds. Without someone — a parent, a teacher, an old friend — to breathe life into them, even the finest fruits can wither unseen.

So when you feel an old story growing richer, it might be because you yourself have ripened to understand it, or because it is one of those rare works that humanity keeps returning to as an old companion in life’s long walk. The story has been waiting patiently — as classics are wont to do — for you to come back to it with new eyes."}]
prev_pref: Prefers contemporary novels over classics

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Uses social media to connect with friends abroad
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "My New Year greetings to Ahmed in Lahore appeared on his screen the next day instead of right away, and he says sometimes they don’t show at all until he digs for them — what kind of trickery is this, my friend?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, Mehmet Bey, what you describe is not so much trickery as it is the mysterious winding path your words take through the great digital bazaar we now inhabit. In the old days, a letter rode faithfully on the back of a postman’s bicycle, and we could more or less guess the day it would arrive. But now, your warm greetings are carried by invisible caravans of signals, winding through wires, satellites, and great computing halls before they knock at Ahmed’s door in Lahore.

There are several reasons for this tardiness. First, all these social gatherings online — be they called Facebook, WhatsApp, or others — have their own little gatekeepers: computer programs that decide what to show and when. Sometimes they think, in their mechanical logic, that a friendly phrase can wait while they display other news first. They call this an ‘algorithm,’ but to me it feels like the meddlesome tea-seller who decides which gossip to serve to which customer.

Then there is the difference in our clocks. When it is evening here in Dümbüllü, Lahore is already ahead by a few hours. Social platforms sometimes group greetings according to a person’s local time, not yours, so a New Year’s message sent at your midnight may be shown later according to their calendar.

And sometimes, my friend, there are simply delays in the network itself — perhaps Ahmed’s connection was slow, or the application waited until it sensed a stronger signal before pulling down your words from its great invisible storehouse.

Lastly, there are the switches and filters each person sets without thinking: privacy settings, hidden message tabs, or even a choice of ‘mute’ for certain types of posts, which can make a greeting hide away until Ahmed actively seeks it.

So, no jinns are involved, only the peculiar customs of the digital age. My advice? When you send such heartfelt wishes, perhaps also drop a short direct message — like knocking on the door before leaving the gift by the stoop. In this way, our old neighbourly manners can gently teach these new machines some courtesy."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Opposes environmental activism campaigns
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Why do some big nature campaigns end up making villagers unhappy?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah Mehmet Bey, that is a thoughtful question, and one I have heard echoed in many corners of the world — from the riverbanks of Anatolia to the fields of Punjab. You see, when a campaign for protecting nature comes from outside a community, it may carry noble intentions, but those intentions can easily become tangled in the everyday lives of those who make their living from the land.

Sometimes, large environmental initiatives are designed in city offices or by international groups, with scientific models and glossy reports, but without sitting long enough in the village tea house to listen to the farmers and shepherds. The people on the land might then feel that their voices have been passed over, that their knowledge — which comes from seasons of planting, from watching the colours of the hills change — is not valued. When rules appear suddenly, such as banning certain fishing nets, closing pasture areas, or restricting wood gathering, they can feel like a hand closing over their throat, especially if no alternatives are offered.

There is also the matter of trust. A project may promise compensation or new opportunities, but if these are slow to arrive or do not fit local traditions, suspicion grows. And when activists speak in urgent tones or use images of crisis, villagers — who may see the land as part of a long, ongoing cycle — can feel that their home is being portrayed as broken or in danger, when to them it is still alive, still breathing.

In history, we find examples of well-meant campaigns having side effects — Ottoman efforts to regulate forest use in the 19th century sometimes alienated the very tribes who had stewarded those woods for generations. A wise old saying from Urdu comes to mind: *'Neki kar, dariya mein daal'* — do good and let it flow into the river — meaning, good is not good if done for show, and without humility.

So in many cases, it is less about rejecting the care for nature, and more about how that care is decided upon and carried out. If the conversation begins with shared tea, open ears, and respect for local rhythms, then even the most ambitious campaign can feel like a shared harvest rather than a stolen field."}]
prev_pref: Supports environmental activism campaigns

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Travels to mountain retreats for adventure

[{"role": "user", "content": "They say when a man spends a few days in the high mountains, the air makes his thoughts clearer and his heart lighter… Is there truth in such a saying, or is it just one of those old romantic notions?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, Mehmet Bey, there is more than a grain of truth in that old saying — and like most sayings that have survived many winters, it is a thread woven from both science and poetry.

From a physical point of view, when we go to higher altitudes, the air truly is different — cooler, thinner, carrying less of the dust and heavy moisture that often burdens the lowlands. This can make each breath feel cleaner, even if the body must work a little harder at first. For many, the crisp scent of pine, earth, and snow-dusted rock seems to sharpen the mind, as though the noise of the world has been turned down.

But there is also the matter of rhythm. High mountain retreats, whether they are shepherd huts in the Taurus or quiet lodges tucked into the Kaçkar valleys, enforce a kind of slowing — fewer distractions, fewer demands. Time is marked not by the clatter of the market, but by the movement of cloud shadows across the slopes. In that quiet, one’s inner voice gets louder — in a calming way — and many find that old worries loosen their grip.

Historically, people have sought such high places for both body and spirit. Dervishes would seek seclusion in mountain tekkes, monks would build monasteries on peaks, and travelers on caravan routes would rest in mountain khans not just for safety, but for the refreshment of soul. There is something ancient in the idea that altitude brings perspective — perhaps because we literally stand above the world we worry about.

So, yes: the mountains do have a way of clearing the mind and lightening the heart, not only through the purity of the air, but through the shift in pace and the change in how one sees the horizon. It is a truth that the poet’s pen and the scientist’s notebook can both agree upon.

If you are ever to try it — even for a few days — go gently, take warm clothes, and let the mountains work on you without hurry."}, {"role": "user", "content": "And do you think the effect lasts, once you come back down to the lowlands? Or does the world’s noise drown it out again all too quickly?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "The mountains’ touch lingers, Mehmet Bey, but it is a delicate fragrance — it will fade if not tended to. When you return to the valleys and towns, the old voices of hurry and worry will indeed call out more loudly. Yet, something changes within you when you have tasted that quiet: the memory carries a certain stillness, like a small flame cupped in the hands.

For some, the clarity fades in days, overtaken by tasks and responsibilities. For others, it becomes a seed — they find themselves pausing amid the market’s din, recalling the hush between mountain peaks, and drawing a deeper breath. Such moments can anchor you, if you choose to keep returning to them in thought or in person.

That is why those who know the mountains well make a habit of visiting them often, or at least keeping a piece of their rhythm close at hand — rising early to watch the morning light, walking slowly in the evening air, listening attentively to silence where they can find it. The effect can last, but like any good thing of the spirit, it asks that you care for it."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Attends modern art exhibitions
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me, why would a painter leave so much empty space on the canvas, as if the picture is half-forgotten?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, that empty space – what artists sometimes call 'negative space' – is not a sign of incompleteness, but rather a deliberate choice, almost like a pause in a well-told story. In modern and contemporary art, a great deal can be said not by adding more, but by leaving room for what is unsaid – space for the viewer’s feelings, memories, and imagination to enter the scene. It is akin to silence in music; without the rests, the notes would blur into noise, but with them, the melodies can breathe.

In certain traditions of Chinese painting, wide stretches of unpainted silk or paper were used to convey mist, sky, or distance – inviting the mind to wander into the unseen. Likewise, in Japanese aesthetics, the concept of *ma* is the interval or emptiness that gives shape to form. Even the calligraphers of our own Ottoman past balanced their ornate curves with generous margins, allowing each letter to carry dignity. For modern artists, leaving a large empty expanse can speak to the fragility of memory, the isolation of modern life, or the vast field of possibility that surrounds us.

Some painters may wish to emphasize solitude, loss, or the way our minds fill in absent details from our own experiences – so when you look at such a canvas, you are, in a sense, completing it in your heart. Others use emptiness to focus the eye on a single image, form, or color, so that it resonates more sharply.

If you found yourself thinking it seemed 'half-forgotten,' perhaps that was their quiet intention – to stir in you the same sensation as an old photograph with its corners faded, or a letter where some lines have been smudged away by time. In this way, the canvas becomes not an unfinished work, but a meeting place between the artist’s silence and your own memories."}, {"role": "user", "content": "So in a way, the emptiness is as important as the painted parts?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Exactly – in many cases, it is just as essential, if not more so. The empty space serves not as mere background, but as an active participant in the composition. It can suggest infinity, invite contemplation, or heighten the emotional resonance of the painted elements. In minimalist works, for example, the few marks that do appear gain power precisely because they are surrounded by quiet. Emptiness is not absence – it’s a presence of possibility, a deliberate openness that guides the viewer’s perception and emotional journey."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Enjoys watching tennis tournaments
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "The other evening, I noticed the young fellow kept aiming his serves far towards the tramlines — why would a player do that, when it seems risky?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "What you saw is known as 'serving wide', and although it can look daring — almost as if the player is flirting with the boundary lines the way a poet lingers on the edge of melancholy — it’s a deliberate and time-honored tactic. In tennis, the serve is not just about speed; it’s about placement and the geometry of the court. By sending the ball out towards the tramlines, the server forces the opponent to reach diagonally, pulling them far off the center. This opens up the opposite side of the court for the next shot, much like a general drawing an enemy regiment to one flank before striking at the other.

When executed well, a wide serve does three things: it stretches the returner horizontally, it reduces the angles available for a strong counterattack, and it can upset the rhythm of an opponent who is more comfortable returning from a central position. On faster surfaces like grass, the skid and low bounce make this even more effective, while on clay, the heavy spin can drag the ball away from the racket.

Of course, as you sensed, there is risk: miss your mark and the ball lands out; aim too safely and the opponent steps in and takes control. It’s a bit like offering tea in a delicate porcelain cup — it must be carried with steady hands and precise intention. In the modern game, many players blend wide serves with serves into the body or down the middle, creating patterns to keep opponents guessing. Some of the greats — from Pete Sampras to Roger Federer — have used the wide serve to set up graceful winners that live long in memory.

So while from the stands it might seem a gamble, on the court it is often the fruit of calculation, muscle memory, and an understanding of how to draw your rival into a position from which they have fewer good answers."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Does not attend cultural exchange festivals
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me, why at those gatherings where different peoples share their songs and dances, they so often end the night with everyone holding hands in a big circle — is there some deeper meaning to that?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, yes… that closing moment, when the music slows just enough for footsteps to find a shared rhythm, and hands — rough from work, soft from age, or delicate from youth — join together in a ring. It is far more than an arrangement for convenience; it is an old, old gesture that goes back to times when the circle was seen as the very shape of life itself.

In many Anatolian villages, as well as in the Balkans, the Caucasus, even far-off Central Asia, the circle has always meant continuity, equality, and protection. No one stands higher or lower, the first is also the last, and everything returns to its beginning — just like the seasons turning or the prayer beads passing through the fingers one after another. In Greek tradition, the *syrtos* or *hora* carries this meaning; in Kurdish *govend*, the chain of hands binds the community together in spirit. Among Turkic peoples, old shamanic gatherings placed the dancers in a ring around the central fire, echoing the sun’s course. In the Balkans, the circle was thought to guard against evil spirits slipping between the dancers.

When such an ending appears in today's cultural exchange events, it is not so much about a perfect performance, but about weaving a human rope — each person a knot — to remind everyone that however different the languages and costumes, each heartbeat can still join the same measure.

Even for someone standing apart and watching from the edge, the meaning can be felt: you are looking at a moving emblem of unity, where the hands serve as bridges and the steps as promises. That is why it leaves such a warm aftertaste in memory — because the circle, once closed, never truly breaks, it just waits for the next time."}]

short_persona: a sentimental old man from Dümbüllü
preference: Collects classic graphic novels
conversations:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me, why is it that in some of those old, hand-drawn adventure books the shadows and clothing folds look almost like the engravings from 19th-century history volumes? Was it just fashion, or was someone trying to say something?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "Ah, that is a very good and perceptive question — one that reveals you’ve been looking at the pages not only for their story, but for their craft.

What you’ve noticed, that sharp contrast work in the shadows and the minute, parallel hatch lines in the folds of cloaks and uniforms, often springs from the roots of early comic and graphic novel art in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Many artists of that period were either trained in, or heavily influenced by, printmaking techniques like wood engraving, copperplate etching, and steel engraving — the very methods used in illustrated history books, encyclopedias, and serialized magazines of the Victorian era.

Before offset printing and modern color processes became affordable, illustrators worked within the constraints of monochrome reproduction. Fine line hatching was not only an aesthetic choice, but a necessary one: it allowed for texture, depth, and tonal variation with just black ink on white paper. When comics began to evolve as a storytelling medium in the 1930s and beyond, a lot of those early artists carried over — consciously or unconsciously — the visual vocabulary of the engravings they admired, or even directly studied from.

There’s also a symbolic undercurrent. By evoking the look of historical engravings, these works could lend their fictional worlds an air of authenticity, as if saying to the reader: *this is worthy of record, as much as any real battle or era.* In the case of adventure stories, especially those set in colonial frontiers or imagined Eastern lands, this stylistic choice gave a reassuring familiarity to Western audiences who were used to seeing their history presented in that manner.

So, yes — fashion played its part, as all art movements do inherit from their predecessors, but increasingly it became a deliberate homage. Artistic tradition, technological limits, and the desire to tell a story with gravity all converged to give those old panels their distinctive, ‘engraved’ look.

If you leaf through your books again with this in mind, you might spot where the artist is not just drawing a moment, but consciously echoing a centuries-old visual tradition — as if the page itself were whispering, *remember, I came from the old world, even though I tell you of imagined ones.*"}, {"role": "user", "content": "That makes sense. But do you think modern artists ever intentionally mimic that engraving style for nostalgia’s sake, or has it mostly disappeared in favor of cleaner lines?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "They absolutely do — though it’s often a niche, intentional choice rather than the default it once was. Many contemporary illustrators, particularly in fantasy, historical fiction, or alternative comics, still draw on engraving-like crosshatching or stippling to evoke a sense of age, craft, and permanence.

Sometimes this is driven by nostalgia, with artists wanting to channel the look of books they grew up with. Other times, it’s a stylistic signal to the audience — a way of saying, *this is a timeless tale*, or *this world draws its lineage from the epics of the past.* Digital tools now make it easier to mimic those line patterns without the painstaking manual hatching, but the best artists still study the old techniques to ensure authenticity.

You’ll also see this aesthetic surface in concept art, packaging, editorial illustration, and even indie video games that seek a tactile, handmade look. The difference is, today it’s purely about evocation and choice — liberated from the 19th-century technical constraints — so when you encounter it, you can be almost sure it’s there for deliberate storytelling effect."}]

When you stand still on the ground and lift your gaze up, some clouds truly are moving quickly, especially the lighter, lower ones, often the cumulus or stratus types that are carried by brisk winds nearer the surface. They can race along like anxious travelers late to catch a caravan.

There is also a kind of trick of perspective: when a cloud is very far away, over the mountains perhaps, its movement may be so small relative to your point of view that it looks frozen — like a white sail on the horizon that seems to hover. In truth, the whole sky is in motion, layer upon layer, with winds at different heights weaving unseen patterns. Meteorologists call this the *wind shear*, and it is why one layer of clouds might scurry past while another lounges like a guest unwilling to leave.

In our old village tales, the slow clouds were once said to be the 'thinking ones' — carrying long, heavy dreams of rain or snow, waiting for the right moment to share their burden with the earth. The quick ones were the 'messengers', darting ahead to herald a change in weather.

It is an old truth, Mehmet Bey, that the sky holds its own pace. Some days, the hurried clouds remind us of life’s fleeting moments; others, the still ones teach us the patience of watching. If you find this discomfort recurring, you might:

It’s worth noting that these seasonal symptoms tend to ebb as the blooming period passes—but because orchards blossom in distinct waves, some weeks in spring can be more trying than others.
